quiz.title: Unit 2: Command of Evidence — Integrating Text and Data (Quiz 6)
quiz.subtitle: Connecting Passage Claims with Quantitative Information (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: SAT Preparation

questions.question:
Ecologists claim that invasive plant species reduce native biodiversity primarily by outcompeting natives for soil nutrients, not by altering habitat structure.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Plot Type} & \text{Native Species Count} & \text{Soil Nitrogen (ppm)} \\ \hline\text{Control (No Invasive)} & 24 & 18 \\ \hline\text{Low Invasive Density} & 19 & 12 \\ \hline\text{High Invasive Density} & 8 & 4 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which choice best describes how the data relates to the ecologists' claim?
options: Native species count and soil nitrogen both decline as invasive density increases.
Habitat structure measurements show greater variation in plots with high invasive density.
Soil nitrogen levels remain stable regardless of invasive plant presence.
Native species count increases when invasive plants are removed from the plot.
questions.explanation: Choice A neutrally describes the parallel decline in both variables, which students must then evaluate against the nutrient-competition claim. Choice B introduces habitat structure, a variable not measured in the table. Choice C contradicts the visible nitrogen decline. Choice D describes an untested intervention (removal) not present in the data.

questions.question:
Economists propose that remote work adoption reduces urban commercial real estate demand but increases suburban residential property values.

Which finding from the market analysis best supports this proposal?
options: Urban office vacancy rates rose from 12% to 31% while suburban home prices increased 18% over the same period.
Commercial property taxes declined in three major cities following the shift to remote work policies.
Suburban municipalities reported increased demand for public transit expansions to accommodate commuter patterns.
Remote workers spend an average of $2,400 annually on home office equipment and high-speed internet upgrades.
questions.explanation: Choice A directly evidences both predicted outcomes: rising urban commercial vacancy and increasing suburban residential values. Choice B addresses only one side (commercial) and focuses on taxes, not demand. Choice C discusses transit infrastructure, not property values. Choice D examines worker spending, not real estate market trends.

questions.question:
A cognitive scientist hypothesizes that multitasking impairs memory encoding not by reducing attention span, but by interfering with the consolidation of information into long-term storage.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Condition} & \text{Immediate Recall (%)} & \text{Delayed Recall (%)} \\ \hline\text{Single-Task} & 84 & 79 \\ \hline\text{Dual-Task} & 81 & 52 \\ \hline\text{Triple-Task} & 78 & 31 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which choice best describes how the data relates to the hypothesis?
options: Immediate recall scores decline slightly as task load increases, while delayed recall drops sharply.
Attention span measurements show no significant difference across the three experimental conditions.
Participants in the triple-task condition reported higher levels of mental fatigue post-experiment.
Memory consolidation rates improve when participants are allowed brief rest periods between tasks.
questions.explanation: Choice A neutrally reports the divergent trends: small immediate-recall changes vs. large delayed-recall declines, which students must evaluate against the consolidation-interference claim. Choice B introduces unmeasured attention-span data. Choice C cites subjective fatigue, not memory performance. Choice D describes an untested intervention (rest periods).

questions.question:
Public health researchers claim that community vaccination campaigns achieve herd immunity faster when targeting high-transmission age groups first, rather than distributing doses evenly across all demographics.

Which outcome from the campaign evaluation best supports this claim?
options: Regions prioritizing adolescents achieved herd immunity thresholds with 42% coverage, while regions using even distribution required 68% coverage.
Vaccine efficacy remained above 90% across all age groups, with no significant variation in immune response by demographic category.
Public trust in vaccination increased by 25% following community-led outreach programs in underserved neighborhoods.
The cost per dose decreased by 15% when vaccines were administered through mobile clinics rather than fixed facilities.
questions.explanation: Choice A directly supports the strategic-targeting claim by showing lower overall coverage achieved herd immunity faster when high-transmission groups were prioritized. Choice B addresses vaccine efficacy, not distribution strategy. Choice C discusses public trust, not transmission dynamics. Choice D focuses on logistical costs, not epidemiological outcomes.

questions.question:
Linguists propose that language change accelerates in communities with high rates of adult second-language acquisition because learners simplify complex grammatical structures, which then spread to native speakers.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Community} & \text{Adult L2 Learners (%)} & \text{Grammatical Simplification Index} \\ \hline\text{Alpha} & 12 & 2.1 \\ \hline\text{Beta} & 38 & 4.7 \\ \hline\text{Gamma} & 61 & 7.9 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which choice best describes how the data relates to the linguists' proposal?
options: Grammatical simplification scores rise as the percentage of adult L2 learners increases across communities.
Communities with greater immigrant populations showed faster lexical innovation but no change in syntactic complexity.
Second-language learners consistently mastered phonological patterns more readily than morphological inflections.
Languages with more complex case systems were acquired more slowly by adult learners than languages with fixed word order.
questions.explanation: Choice A neutrally describes the positive correlation between L2 learner percentage and simplification index, which students must evaluate against the proposed transmission mechanism. Choice B addresses lexical change, not grammatical simplification. Choice C describes acquisition patterns without linking them to community-level change. Choice D compares language features without addressing the spread of simplifications.

questions.question:
A marine biologist claims that coral bleaching events are driven more by prolonged temperature anomalies than by short-term heat spikes.

Which finding from the study best supports this claim?
options: Reefs exposed to +2°C for eight weeks showed 90% bleaching, whereas reefs experiencing +4°C spikes for three days showed less than 15% bleaching.
Water temperature fluctuations in the study region ranged from 1°C to 5°C above seasonal norms during the observation period.
Coral species with thicker tissue layers demonstrated greater resistance to thermal stress across all temperature conditions.
Bleaching severity correlated strongly with light intensity, suggesting solar radiation amplifies temperature effects.
questions.explanation: Choice A directly contrasts prolonged moderate anomalies with brief extreme spikes, supporting the claim about duration over intensity. Choice B describes temperature ranges without linking them to bleaching outcomes. Choice C introduces a biological variable (tissue thickness) unrelated to the duration claim. Choice D shifts focus to light intensity, a confounding factor not central to the hypothesis.

questions.question:
Educational researchers argue that handwriting lecture notes improves long-term memory retention by forcing students to synthesize information rather than transcribe it verbatim.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Note-Taking Method} & \text{Verbatim Recall (%)} & \text{Conceptual Recall (%)} \\ \hline\text{Handwritten} & 41 & 68 \\ \hline\text{Typed} & 73 & 49 \\ \hline\text{Audio Recording} & 38 & 33 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which choice best describes how the data relates to the researchers' argument?
options: Handwritten notes yield lower verbatim recall but higher conceptual recall compared to typed notes.
Audio recording produces the lowest scores on both verbatim and conceptual recall measures.
Typed notes result in higher verbatim recall because typing speed exceeds handwriting speed.
Students prefer handwritten notes for humanities courses and typed notes for STEM courses.
questions.explanation: Choice A neutrally reports the trade-off pattern: handwritten notes sacrifice verbatim accuracy for conceptual understanding, which students must evaluate against the synthesis claim. Choice B describes audio recording performance but doesn't address the handwritten-vs-typed comparison central to the argument. Choice C introduces typing speed, an unmeasured variable. Choice D cites course preferences, not memory outcomes.

questions.question:
Urban planners hypothesize that expanding municipal green spaces reduces resident stress levels by providing accessible environments for physiological recovery.

Which finding would provide the most relevant evidence for this hypothesis?
options: Cortisol levels decreased by 18% in residents living within 300 meters of parks compared to those living further away.
Property values in neighborhoods adjacent to parks increased by 12% following recent landscaping projects.
Municipal trees absorb approximately 48 pounds of carbon dioxide annually, contributing to cleaner air.
One community member reported feeling noticeably relaxed after walking through the central botanical garden.
questions.explanation: Choice A provides direct physiological data linking proximity to green spaces with reduced stress markers, directly testing the hypothesized mechanism. Choice B addresses economic impact, not stress physiology. Choice C discusses environmental benefits unrelated to human stress responses. Choice D relies on a single anecdote rather than systematic evidence.

questions.question:
A historian argues that the construction of transcontinental railroads in the 19th century accelerated economic integration more by standardizing time zones than by reducing transport costs.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Metric} & \text{1870} & \text{1890} \\ \hline\text{Avg. Freight Cost (per ton-mile)} & 2.8\textcent & 1.7\textcent \\ \hline\text{Municipalities on Standard Time} & 12 & 318 \\ \hline\text{Interstate Commerce Volume} & \$42\text{M} & \$217\text{M} \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which choice best describes how the data relates to the historian's argument?
options: Standard-time adoption increased 26-fold while freight costs declined by less than half over the same period.
Freight cost reductions correlate strongly with increases in interstate commerce volume across all regions.
Municipalities that adopted standard time earlier showed faster economic growth than those that delayed adoption.
Transport cost declines preceded widespread time-zone standardization by approximately five years.
questions.explanation: Choice A neutrally compares the magnitude of change in the two variables central to the argument, which students must evaluate against the claim that time-zone standardization had greater impact. Choice B focuses only on freight costs, ignoring the time-zone variable. Choice C introduces economic growth, an outcome not directly measured in the table. Choice D makes a temporal claim about sequencing not verifiable from the two time points.

questions.question:
Nutritionists claim that replacing red meat with plant-based proteins reduces systemic inflammation by lowering saturated fat intake and increasing antioxidant consumption.

Which finding from the clinical trial best supports this claim?
options: Blood tests revealed a 22% reduction in inflammatory markers among participants who replaced red meat with legumes for eight weeks.
Industrial livestock production generates significant methane emissions, accelerating climate change.
Plant-based groceries often cost 15% more than conventional meat products at suburban supermarkets.
Many individuals report disliking the texture of lentils and prefer traditional beef preparations.
questions.explanation: Choice A provides clinical biomarker data directly measuring the claimed physiological mechanism (reduced inflammation). Choice B addresses environmental impact, not human health outcomes. Choice C discusses market pricing, irrelevant to biological effects. Choice D cites personal taste preferences, not scientific evidence.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can accurately connect textual claims with quantitative evidence and evaluate whether data supports specific mechanisms.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review missed questions and focus on whether the data directly addresses the mechanism or outcome stated in the claim.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Remember: factual accuracy is not enough. Evidence must be directly relevant to the specific argument being made.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Ask yourself: does this data measure the exact variable or relationship the claim describes?
